key_insight: Hydrogen segregation near a screw dislocation in bcc iron is governed by core structure and site-specific trapping, and a thermodynamic framework can reduce this complex landscape to the most statistically relevant configurations.
engineering_relevance: The results provide a multiscale basis for modeling hydrogen embrittlement and plasticity in iron, including when simplified elastic-dipole descriptions are valid.
method: Systematic molecular simulations with a neural-network interatomic potential
Mapped hydrogen binding across easy-core and hard-core dislocation structures and multiple site types/concentrations
Built a thermodynamic model to rank trapping configurations and compare with simplified elastic dipole theory
evidence_signal: The simulation-based energetics show good agreement with the limited DFT data available in the literature and help explain experimental hydrogen solubility limits.
watch_item: The simplified elastic-dipole description is only valid within a limited domain, so it should not be assumed accurate for all dislocation core states or hydrogen concentrations.
paper_title: Hydrogen segregation around a straight screw dislocation in bcc iron
